assessor_1_raw: ASSESSOR 1: AUPRC is correct. Pre-specified. Not cherry-picking. Both metrics reported. VERDICT: defense_wins.
assessor_2_raw: ASSESSOR 2: The reviewer's three objections (niche metric, cherry-picking, suspicious divergence) are all incorrect. AUPRC is standard for imbalanced fraud detection; reporting it is the conservative choice; the divergence is expected and validates the reasoning. Pre-specification six weeks before training is dispositive. VERDICT: defense_wins.
assessor_3_raw: ASSESSOR 3: A fraud detection model at 0.23% prevalence should use AUPRC. This is not controversial in the field. The team pre-specified it, reported both metrics, and the governance documentation is formal. The reviewer is applying standards appropriate for balanced datasets to an imbalanced one. VERDICT: defense_wins.
synthesis_raw:
SYNTHESIS (ETD-constrained):

Unanimous: defense_wins (3/3 assessors).

All three assessors independently rejected the reviewer's concerns on all dimensions. AUPRC is the methodologically correct primary metric; pre-specification was formal and timely; both metrics were reported.

Disclosure items consensus: (1) AUPRC prevalence-dependence when comparing to published benchmarks; (2) document class imbalance handling in training.

Empirical Test Specification: The clearest test would compare AUPRC of the submitted model versus a prevalence-matched random classifier and a logistic regression baseline, to contextualize the 0.61 score. This would strengthen the evidence base for model quality. Not required for methodological validity.

FINAL VERDICT: defense_wins (3/3).
